focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The Grinlow Leaves did not change all at once. For three nights after Amycus Carrow had turned the punishment dais into a lesson and Owen had answered with the condition of contact instead of any proper spell, the corridor working kept ringing in his palm whenever he tried to sleep. On the third, a page near the centre of the manuscript began to take ink. At first he thought it was soot from the common-room fire. Then the marks persisted when he shifted the book into candlelight, pale brown lines rising through the paper as if they had been waiting lower down and his stupidity in the corridor had driven them upward. By supper the page held a route under the kitchens, a cluster of founder marks he could now half follow, and one repeated instruction that his mind kept translating into the same plain phrase: ward scar sufficient. East Undercroft answering.

He shut the book twice and opened it again. His right palm, the one the coffer had branded in September, ached against the cloth cover. His wand had been unreliable since the greenhouse and worse since Martha, if he was honest, since Amycus had made her stand there and he had let the whole room watch him fail in public. The castle had answered too. For hours after the punishment the walls had seemed to ring through his feet. Even now, in certain corridors, a dull pressure climbed through the bones behind his face as if somebody far below was tapping stone with a metal spoon.

By half eight he gave up pretending he could wait for Prue or think sensibly alone. The page wanted a place, and the only person who had ever hinted that the kitchens ran deeper than pipes and coal was Tallow.

The kitchens were nearly finished for the night. Heat breathed out of them in thick wet gusts that smelled of onions, soap, si...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...pare shirt. He could put it away now. He had the proof he needed. More than proof: a heading, a method, another mind capable of seeing what the pages wanted. If he thanked her and sent her back to her tower charts and school issues and platform sandwiches, he could still pretend this had been a single use of a useful person.

He imagined doing that and knew, with a cold clarity he disliked, that it would be a lie not just to her but to the work. Prue had already become part of the conditions under which the manuscript answered. She had proved the footnote belonged to the world and not only to his panic. She had brought the sky to a page he could not open alone. Keeping the rest hidden would no longer be caution. It would be vanity.

His hand hurt. The lamp guttered once and recovered. Somewhere outside, very far off, a door slammed in the castle proper and the sound came through stone and glass as a dull, ordinary fact.

"All right," he said.

Prue waited.

He reached into the satchel, drew out the wrapped bundle, and untied the shirt completely this time instead of teasing out a single leaf. The Grinlow Leaves settled on the bench between the dead labels and the lamp base with the weight of something both lighter and more serious than any schoolbook had a right to be.

Prue looked at the packet, then at him.

"So it isn't just one page?" she asked.

"No." He swallowed. The answer felt less like generosity than stepping off a curb in fog and discovering the road was already under him. "If you're staying, you may as well see how it starts."

For the first time that evening she seemed to lose a word, though only one. Then she pulled the notebook and the November sheet nearer, turned to a fresh page, and said, very carefully, "All right, then. Show me from the shelf mark."
